sanskrit:
द्वाविमौ पुरुषौ लोके क्षरश्चाक्षर एव च |
क्षरः सर्वाणि भूतानि कूटस्थोऽक्षर उच्यते ॥१६॥
iast:
dvāvimau puruṣau loke kṣaraścākṣara eva ca |
kṣaraḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni kūṭastho'kṣara ucyate ||16||
explanation: There are two types of beings (purushas) in the world — the perishable (kshara) and the imperishable (akshara). All material beings are perishable; the unchanging is called imperishable.
synthesis: There are two purushas in the world — the perishable and the imperishable. All beings are the perishable; the unchanging essence is called the imperishable. Shankara distinguishes the ever-changing phenomenal world from the unchanging witness-consciousness. Ramanuja identifies the perishable as the body-matter complex and the imperishable as the individual soul. The Bhakti tradition sees this distinction as preparation for understanding the Supreme who transcends both. Madhva explains these as genuinely distinct categories — perishable beings subject to change, and the imperishable unchanging essence of souls — with God transcending both. Abhinavagupta reads them as two aspects of consciousness: the kshara identified with changing forms, and the akshara in its undifferentiated state. Vallabha teaches that both are real and divine, but God as Purushottama transcends both as the complete reality. Tilak finds this essential: the world of action is real, eternal principles are unchanging, and the Supreme gives ultimate meaning to both. Vivekananda sees this as the Gita's clearest philosophical framework: change, changelessness, and the Absolute beyond both.

commentaries.text: Shankara identifies kshara as the totality of manifest beings subject to change, and akshara as maya or the seed-power of the unmanifest — the causal state from which creation emerges and into which it dissolves. Both are within the realm of Brahman's apparent manifestation. The kutastha akshara is the unchanging witness-consciousness in each being.

Ramanuja explains kshara as all individual souls in their embodied, conditioned state, and akshara as those same souls in their liberated, essential nature — unchanged and eternal. Both the bound and the liberated soul are real categories, and both are distinct from the Supreme Purusha who will be introduced in verse 18.

The bhakti tradition uses this verse to distinguish between the passing world of material experience and the eternal soul that witnesses it all. Everything you can see, touch, and lose is kshara. The part of you that has never changed — your deepest witness — is akshara. But the Beloved Lord is beyond both, as the next verses will reveal.

Madhva explains the two purushas — perishable (kshara) and imperishable (akshara) — as genuinely distinct categories. The perishable includes all individual beings subject to change; the imperishable is the unchanging essence of souls. God as Purushottama transcends both categories entirely.

Abhinavagupta reads the two purushas as two aspects of consciousness: the kshara is consciousness identified with changing forms (the world), and the akshara is consciousness in its unchanging, undifferentiated state. Both are modes of the one reality, which in its fullness is Purushottama — the supreme Shiva.

Vallabha teaches that the perishable (all created beings) and the imperishable (their eternal essence) are both real and both divine. But God as Purushottama transcends both — He is the complete reality of which perishable and imperishable are partial expressions. This is the hierarchy of divine being.

Tilak finds this verse essential for the karma yogi's worldview: the world of action (kshara) is real and important; the eternal principles behind it (akshara) are unchanging. But the Supreme Person who transcends both gives ultimate meaning to all activity — duty connects the changing to the changeless.

Vivekananda sees this as the Gita's clearest philosophical framework: everything changes (kshara), something never changes (akshara), and the Supreme transcends both categories. This threefold understanding — change, changelessness, and the Absolute beyond both — is the complete metaphysical picture.

applications.personal_growth: Learning to distinguish between what changes (moods, circumstances, age, roles) and what remains constant (your core awareness, deepest values) is fundamental to inner stability. When you identify with the unchanging rather than the changing, peace becomes your natural state.
applications.career_business: In business, understanding what is perishable (products, trends, technologies) versus imperishable (principles, reputation, relationships) helps you invest wisely. Companies that cling to perishable advantages fail; those built on imperishable foundations adapt and endure.
applications.health: Your body is kshara — always changing, aging, regenerating. Your awareness of the body is akshara — the constant observer. Health anxiety decreases dramatically when you learn to rest in the unchanging witness rather than identifying solely with the changing body.
applications.relationships: Every relationship has perishable elements (physical attraction, shared circumstances, daily habits) and imperishable ones (deep connection, shared values, soul-level resonance). Knowing the difference helps you hold change gracefully without mistaking it for loss.

sanskrit:
उत्तमः पुरुषस्त्वन्यः परमात्मेत्युदाहृतः |
यो लोकत्रयमाविश्य बिभर्त्यव्यय ईश्वरः ॥१७॥
iast:
uttamaḥ puruṣastvanyaḥ paramātmetyudāhṛtaḥ |
yo lokatrayamāviśya bibhartyavyaya īśvaraḥ ||17||
explanation: But there is another, the Supreme Person, called the Paramatma (Supreme Self), the imperishable Lord who pervades and sustains the three worlds.
synthesis: But there is another, the Supreme Person (Purushottama), the imperishable Lord who enters the three worlds and sustains them. Shankara identifies Purushottama with the supreme Brahman beyond all dualities. Ramanuja sees this as the Lord who is distinct from and superior to both matter and souls. The Bhakti tradition worships Purushottama as Krishna — the personal God who transcends and sustains all. Madhva explains that Purushottama is utterly distinct from both the perishable and imperishable — the definitive statement of God's absolute supremacy. Abhinavagupta reads Purushottama as Paramashiva — the absolute consciousness that includes and transcends both dynamic and static aspects. Vallabha identifies Purushottama as Krishna Himself, the Supreme Person who encompasses but exceeds both categories. Tilak sees this as establishing the supreme reference point for all action — ultimate loyalty belongs to the Supreme Person. Vivekananda interprets Purushottama as the highest conception of reality, harmonizing personal and impersonal conceptions of God.

commentaries.text: Shankara interprets the Uttama Purusha as Nirguna Brahman — the absolute reality that transcends all distinctions including kshara and akshara. Being called 'Paramatma' here means the supreme Self that is the true identity of all beings. The three worlds are pervaded and sustained by this one reality, which appears as many due to upadhis (limiting adjuncts).

Ramanuja identifies the Supreme Person as Narayana — distinct from both bound souls (kshara) and liberated souls (akshara), yet intimately present within all as their inner controller. The word 'anyah' proves that God is a distinct, supreme personality — not merely the collective totality of all souls but their sovereign Lord who pervades and sustains everything through His will.

The bhakti tradition hears this verse as the revelation of the Beloved's true nature: He is not just a force or principle but a Supreme Person who lovingly enters all creation to sustain it. He is 'another' — unique, beyond comparison — yet He pervades everything intimately. The devotee loves this Person, not an abstraction.

Madhva explains that Purushottama (the Supreme Person) is utterly distinct from both the perishable and the imperishable — He is the transcendent Lord who enters and sustains all three worlds. This is the definitive statement of God's absolute supremacy over all other categories of reality.

Abhinavagupta reads Purushottama as Paramashiva — the absolute consciousness that includes and transcends both the dynamic (kshara) and the static (akshara) aspects of reality. He enters the three worlds not by moving but by being the awareness in which all worlds appear.

Vallabha identifies Purushottama as Krishna Himself — the Supreme Person whose nature encompasses but exceeds both the perishable world and the imperishable soul. He sustains all three worlds through His omnipresent being. This is the ultimate object of devotion in pushti marga.

Tilak sees this as establishing the supreme reference point for all action: the karma yogi's ultimate loyalty is not to the changing world or even to unchanging principles but to the Supreme Person who transcends both. This gives action its deepest possible grounding and purpose.

Vivekananda interprets Purushottama as the highest conception of reality — beyond both the phenomenal world and the abstract absolute. This teaching harmonizes the personal and impersonal conceptions of God, showing that the ultimate reality includes but transcends both categories.

applications.personal_growth: Beyond your changing moods and your unchanging awareness, there may be a deeper intelligence at work in your life — a sustaining presence that you didn't create and can't fully comprehend. Openness to this 'something beyond' is the beginning of genuine spiritual life.
applications.career_business: The greatest leaders operate from a level beyond both daily operations (perishable) and fixed strategy (imperishable). They have a quality of presence and vision that pervades and sustains the entire organization — something that transcends both tactics and principles.
applications.health: Beyond the changing body and the unchanging life-force, there is an intelligence that coordinates trillions of cells, heals wounds, and maintains balance. Trusting this deeper organizing intelligence — and cooperating with it rather than overriding it — is the highest form of health wisdom.
applications.relationships: The deepest relationships are sustained by something beyond both partners — a 'third presence' that emerges when two people genuinely open to each other. This transcendent quality in a relationship is what people mean when they say 'our love is bigger than both of us.'

यस्मात्क्षरमतीतोऽहमक्षरादपि चोत्तमः |
अतोऽस्मि लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः ॥१८॥
iast:
yasmātkṣaramatīto'hamakṣarādapi cottamaḥ |
ato'smi loke vede ca prathitaḥ puruṣottamaḥ ||18||
explanation: Because I transcend the perishable and am beyond even the imperishable, I am celebrated in the world and in the Vedas as the Purushottama — the Supreme Person.
synthesis: Because I transcend the perishable and am beyond even the imperishable, I am celebrated in the world and in the Vedas as the Supreme Person (Purushottama). Shankara sees this as Brahman declaring its transcendence of all categories. Ramanuja reads it as the Lord affirming His supreme status as the ground of all existence. The Bhakti tradition celebrates this as Krishna's ultimate self-revelation. Madhva teaches that this is not mere title but a description of God's actual metaphysical supremacy. Abhinavagupta sees this as the ultimate self-declaration: consciousness transcending all its own manifestations and even its unmanifest ground. Vallabha explains that Krishna being Purushottama confirms the personal God as the highest truth. Tilak reads 'celebrated in the world and the Vedas' as meaning the Supreme is recognized through both scripture and lived experience. Vivekananda interprets this as affirming that the highest truth is both scripturally grounded and experientially verifiable — a universal reality.

commentaries.text: Shankara interprets Purushottama as the supreme Brahman in its aspect as Ishvara — the personal God who is, from the ultimate standpoint, identical with Nirguna Brahman. Transcending both kshara and akshara means that Brahman is neither the world of change nor the principle of changelessness, but the non-dual reality in which both appear. The name 'Purushottama' is a concession to devotional understanding of the attributeless Absolute.

Ramanuja takes this as the supreme theological statement of the Gita: the Lord is Purushottama — categorically superior to all beings, whether bound or liberated. This establishes the core Vishishtadvaita principle: God, souls, and matter are all real, but God is supremely different and infinitely greater. The Vedas exist to reveal Him, and the world exists within Him.

For devotees, this verse is the supreme revelation: the Ultimate Reality is not an impersonal void but a Supreme Person — one who can be loved, known, served, and surrendered to. Krishna is not just a teacher or avatar but the Purushottama — the highest being in all existence. The devotee's heart leaps: the one I love is the Absolute itself.

Madhva teaches that Krishna's transcendence of both the perishable and the imperishable establishes Him as the Purushottama celebrated in the Vedas and by the wise. This is not mere title but a description of God's actual metaphysical status as the supreme reality.

Abhinavagupta sees this as the ultimate self-declaration of consciousness: I transcend all my own manifestations (kshara) and even my own unmanifest ground (akshara). The name Purushottama points to awareness in its absolute fullness — the state beyond all states that is the true nature of reality.

Vallabha explains that Krishna being celebrated as Purushottama in both scripture and the world confirms that the personal God is the highest truth. The impersonal Brahman is an aspect of His being, not the reverse. This is the definitive statement of theistic Vedanta.

Tilak reads 'celebrated in the world and in the Vedas' as indicating that the Supreme Person is recognized not only in scripture but in the living experience of those who act with devotion. The karma yogi encounters Purushottama through dedicated service, not through scholarship alone.

Vivekananda interprets this as the Gita's affirmation that the highest truth is both scripturally grounded and experientially verifiable. Purushottama is not a sectarian concept but a universal reality known to all traditions that have probed deeply enough into the nature of existence.

applications.personal_growth: The highest aspiration is not to become nothing (pure detachment) or to become everything (pure ambition) but to connect with something greater than both — a supreme reality that includes and transcends all opposites. The deepest fulfillment comes from relationship with the Ultimate.
applications.career_business: The most admired leaders and brands are not those who are merely excellent at execution (perishable advantage) or those with timeless principles (imperishable advantage) — they are those who embody something that transcends both: a living, dynamic quality that people recognize as genuinely supreme.
applications.health: Optimal health is not about maximizing performance (perishable) or achieving perfect balance (imperishable) — it is about being so connected to the deepest source of vitality that both performance and balance arise naturally. The supreme state of health transcends both striving and stability.
applications.relationships: The greatest love is not passionate intensity (perishable) or steady commitment (imperishable) — it is a love that transcends and includes both, something people universally recognize as extraordinary. Such love is celebrated precisely because it reveals something beyond the ordinary human range.

sanskrit:
यो मामेवमसम्मूढो जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम् |
स सर्वविद्भजति मां सर्वभावेन भारत ॥१९॥
iast:
yo māmevamasammūḍho jānāti puruṣottamam |
sa sarvavidbhajati māṃ sarvabhāvena bhārata ||19||
explanation: One who, without delusion, knows Me as the Purushottama (Supreme Person) — that person, knowing everything, worships Me with their whole being, O Bharata.
synthesis: One who, without delusion, knows Me as Purushottama — that all-knowing person worships Me with their entire being. Shankara sees this as the culmination of jnana yoga — complete knowledge dissolving into complete devotion. Ramanuja reads it as knowledge and devotion united in the realization of God's supreme nature. The Bhakti tradition celebrates the undeluded devotee whose every breath is worship. Madhva teaches that knowing Krishna as Purushottama is the culmination of all understanding — not partial knowledge but complete wisdom. Abhinavagupta sees this as the supreme recognition — undeluded awareness recognizing its own nature as the absolute, with every act becoming self-recognition. Vallabha interprets this as the fulfillment of pushti marga: total devotion flowing from complete divine knowledge. Tilak reads 'worships Me in every way' as the karma yogi's ideal: every action becomes worship when performed with knowledge of the Supreme. Vivekananda emphasizes that genuine knowledge dispels all confusion, and the person who truly knows worships through every act of life.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that knowing Purushottama means realizing the non-dual Brahman as one's own Self and the Self of all. Such a person is 'sarvavit' because Brahman-knowledge encompasses all knowledge. 'Worshipping with the whole being' means every action, thought, and experience becomes an expression of this realization — there is no longer a division between sacred and secular.

Ramanuja interprets 'knowing everything' as knowing the essential truth: the Supreme Person's nature, attributes, and relationship to all beings. This knowledge, far from being dry, ignites all-consuming devotion. 'Sarvabhavena' means with every emotion, every thought, every breath — the devotee's entire being becomes oriented toward the Lord in loving service.

The bhakti tradition sees this as the description of prema-bhakti — the highest love. When you truly know someone as the Supreme Person, love is not a choice but an overflow. The devotee who knows Krishna as Purushottama doesn't 'practice' devotion — devotion pours from every pore. Their whole being sings, serves, and surrenders.

Madhva teaches that one who knows Krishna as Purushottama without delusion is a true knower who worships God with complete devotion in every way. This is not partial knowledge but the culmination of all understanding — knowing God as supreme over all categories of reality.

Abhinavagupta sees 'knowing Me as Purushottama' as the supreme recognition (pratyabhijna) — the undeluded awareness that recognizes its own nature as the absolute consciousness beyond all categories. This knower 'worships Me in every way' because every act of awareness is now seen as an act of self-recognition.

Vallabha interprets this as the fulfillment of pushti marga: the devotee who knows Krishna as Purushottama — without any doubt or delusion — naturally worships Him with every fiber of being. This total devotion is the sign of complete divine knowledge and the prerequisite for liberation.

Tilak reads 'worships Me in every way' as the karma yogi's ideal: every action becomes worship when performed with the knowledge that the Supreme Person pervades all. Work, family, service — everything becomes an offering to Purushottama when this knowledge is alive.

Vivekananda emphasizes 'without delusion' — genuine knowledge dispels all confusion about the nature of reality. The person who truly knows the Supreme worships through every act of life — eating, working, loving, serving — because they see the divine in everything.

applications.personal_growth: When you truly understand something at the deepest level, it changes not just your thinking but your entire way of being. The difference between intellectual knowledge and transformative knowledge is that the latter reorganizes your whole life around its truth.
applications.career_business: When someone finds their true calling — the work that aligns with their deepest nature — they don't just do it with their skills; they do it with their whole being. This total engagement is the hallmark of mastery and the source of extraordinary impact.
applications.health: The healthiest people don't just follow health rules — they have internalized wellness as a way of being. Their whole life — sleep, food, movement, thought, connection — is an expression of vitality. Health becomes not a goal but a natural state.
applications.relationships: When you truly know someone — their depth, their beauty, their divine nature — love becomes total. Not obsessive, but complete. You love them with your thoughts, your attention, your actions, your patience, your whole being. This is what lasting love looks like.

sanskrit:
इति गुह्यतमं शास्त्रमिदमुक्तं मयानघ |
एतद्बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान्स्यात्कृतकृत्यश्च भारत ॥२०॥
iast:
iti guhyatamaṃ śāstramidamuktaṃ mayānagha |
etadbuddhvā buddhimānsyātkṛtakṛtyaśca bhārata ||20||
explanation: Thus, this most secret teaching has been revealed by Me, O sinless one. Understanding this, a person becomes truly wise and has accomplished all that needs to be accomplished, O Bharata.
synthesis: Thus this most secret teaching has been revealed by Me, O sinless one. Understanding this, a person becomes truly wise and has accomplished all that is to be accomplished. Shankara sees this as the Gita confirming that knowledge of Purushottama is the highest wisdom. Ramanuja reads it as God's assurance that this teaching contains everything needed for liberation. The Bhakti tradition treasures this as the most intimate teaching reserved for the purest devotees. Madhva concludes that this reveals God's supreme status as Purushottama — the capstone of the Gita's theology. Abhinavagupta reads the 'most secret teaching' as the final revelation of consciousness to itself — recognizing what has always been true. Vallabha teaches that knowing Krishna as the Supreme Person beyond both perishable and imperishable is the most intimate revelation. Tilak sees this as declaring that true wisdom naturally leads to right action — the enlightened karma yogi has accomplished everything. Vivekananda reads this as the Gita's assurance that understanding the Supreme brings complete fulfillment — clear seeing of ultimate reality is what makes a person truly wise.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that this teaching is 'most secret' because it reveals the ultimate truth hidden behind all other teachings — the identity of the Self with Brahman as Purushottama. One who realizes this has nothing more to know, do, or attain. 'Krita-kritya' means all spiritual debts are paid, all purposes fulfilled, all seeking ended in the stillness of Self-realization.

Ramanuja emphasizes that this is the most confidential teaching because it reveals what even other Gita chapters only partially disclose: the Supreme Person who transcends both matter and soul. Understanding this means understanding the goal of all scripture. The person becomes 'krita-kritya' — fully accomplished — because knowing the Purushottama fulfills the purpose of all dharma.

For the bhakti tradition, this verse seals the chapter with a kiss: Krishna calls Arjuna 'sinless one' (anagha) and shares His deepest secret — that He is the Supreme Person, the goal of all paths. The devotee who receives this teaching with an open heart has nothing left to achieve. Life becomes not a striving but a celebration of the Beloved's presence.

Madhva concludes that this most secret teaching reveals God's supreme status as Purushottama. The person who understands this has fulfilled the purpose of all knowledge and all scripture. Madhva sees this as the capstone of the Gita's theology — God's absolute supremacy over all.

Abhinavagupta reads the 'most secret teaching' as the final revelation of consciousness to itself: you are the Purushottama, the supreme awareness beyond all categories. Understanding this is not gaining new information but recognizing what has always been true. This recognition is the accomplishment of all accomplishments.

Vallabha teaches that this chapter's secret teaching — that Krishna is the Supreme Person beyond both the perishable and imperishable — is the most intimate revelation of the Gita. The devotee who understands this has found the goal of all seeking and becomes truly wise through God's grace.

Tilak sees this final verse as declaring that true wisdom — understanding the Purushottama — naturally leads to right action. The enlightened karma yogi has accomplished everything worth accomplishing because they act from the deepest possible understanding of reality's nature.

Vivekananda reads this as the Gita's assurance that genuine understanding of the Supreme brings complete fulfillment. Not material success, not scholarly reputation, but the clear seeing of ultimate reality — this is what makes a person truly accomplished and wise.

applications.personal_growth: There comes a point in personal development where you realize you have enough knowledge, enough tools, enough insight. The task shifts from gathering more to embodying what you already know. This is the 'krita-kritya' moment — recognizing that the essential work is done.
applications.career_business: Career fulfillment is not an endless escalator. At some point, a wise professional recognizes: I have accomplished what truly matters. This doesn't mean retirement but a shift from striving to contributing — from 'what more can I get?' to 'how can I share what I know?'
applications.health: Health wisdom reaches a completion point when you understand the fundamentals deeply enough that no new diet, supplement, or trend can shake your foundation. You become 'krita-kritya' in health — grounded, complete, no longer searching for the next miracle cure.
applications.relationships: The deepest relational wisdom is knowing when the learning is complete — when you've understood enough about love, communication, and commitment to simply live it. This is the transition from relationship 'work' to relationship joy.
